catalyst.source: In this study, a series of Z-scheme BiOIg-C 3 N 4 photocatalysts are synthesized and their photocatalytic performance for CO 2 reduction to produce CO, H 2 andor CH 4 is evaluated under visible light irradiation ( 400 nm).
catalyst.context: Rational design and construction of Z-scheme photocatalysts has received much attention in the field of CO 2 reduction because of its great potential to solve the current energy and environmental crises.In this study, a series of Z-scheme BiOIg-C 3 N 4 photocatalysts are synthesized and their photocatalytic performance for CO 2 reduction to produce CO, H 2 andor CH 4 is evaluated under visible light irradiation ( 400 nm).The results show that the as-synthesized composites exhibit more highly efficient photocatalytic activity than pure g-C 3 N 4 and BiOI and that the product yields change remarkably depending on the reaction conditions such as irradiation light wavelength.Emphasis is placed on identifying how the charge transfers across the heterojunctions and an indirect Z-scheme charge transfer mechanism is verified by detecting the intermediate I 3-ions.The reaction mechanism is further proposed based on the detection of the intermediate OH and H 2 O 2 .This work may be useful for rationally designing of new types of Z-scheme photocatalyst and provide some illuminating insights into the Z-scheme transfer mechanism.

catalyst.source: The hybridized BiOIg-C 3 N 4 composites were synthesized by a simple deposition method.
catalyst.context: 2.1.Catalyst Preparation.All chemicals used in our experiments were of analytical reagent grade without further purification.Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C 3 N 4 ) was prepared according to the literature method. 47The hybridized BiOIg-C 3 N 4 composites were synthesized by a simple deposition method.In a typical experimental procedure, a quantitative of Bi(NO 3 ) 3 5H 2 O and g-C 3 N 4 (0.170 g) was added to 70.0 mL of glycol, according to the weight contents of BiOI in the composites with the percentage of 5, 8, 10, 11, 15, and 40 wt, respectively.After the suspension was ultrasonically dispersed for 30 min, a solution of KI in 95 alcohol (0.050 molL -1 ) was added dropwise and stirred for 24 h at room temperature.Samples with different ratio of BiOIg-C 3 N 4 were thus obtained after drying at 60 C for 12 h.To verify the real ratio of BiOIg-C 3 N 4 in the final composites, thermogravimetric analysis was employed to determine the content of BiOI.The results are displayed in Figure S1 and Table S1.The calculated BiOI contents are 2.5, 5.2, 7.4, 10.1, 14.3, and 38.9 wt , respectively.The calculated values are smaller than the nominal ones, indicating that a small part of BiOI was lost in the deposition process.The resulting specimens are labeled as x-BiOIg-C 3 N 4 , where x stands for the BiOI wt in the samples.For comparison, we also prepared pure BiOI and g-C 3 N 4 under the same conditions. 2.2.Catalyst Characterization.Thermal decomposition of the x-BiOIg-C 3 N 4 composites was monitored by thermogravimetric and differential scanning calorimeter (TG-DSC) with instrument NETZSCH STA 449C in a flow of air with heating rate of 5 C min in alumina crucible up to 750 C.The crystal phases of the samples were recorded with X-ray diffractometer (PANalytical X' pert PRO, Netherlands).Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was obtained on a Tecnai G 2 F20 S-TWIN electron microscope.Further, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) was employed.The PL spectra of samples were detected using a Hitachi F-4600 spectrometer.Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms were collected on a NOVA 1000e surface area and porosity analyzer (Quantachrome, Boynton Beach, FL). Chemical components and valence states were analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) on a Thermo Scientific ESCALab 250Xi Spectrometer with Al K radiation as the excitation source.Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) data was obtained with HeI (21.22 eV) as monochromatic light source.UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were performed on a scan UV-vis spectrometer (Cary 5000) with integrating sphere detector.The Raman spectra were obtained using a Renishaw inVia spectrophotometer.Photoelectrochemical tests in a conventional three-electrode system were recorded by a CHI 660E electrochemical workstation (Chenhua, Shanghai, China).The photocurrents of the photocatalysts were measured at 0.0 V (vs.AgAgCl) in Na 2 SO 4 aqueous solution under UV-visible light with AM 1.5 filter after being purged by N 2 to remove O 2 . 2.3.Photocatalytic Performance Tests.Photocatalytic activity for CO 2 reduction of the synthesized catalysts was evaluated in gaseous phase in a stainless steel cylindrical vessel with 15 cm length and 180 mL volume.The light source was a 300 W xenon arc lamp (PLS-SXE 300, Beijing Trust tech Co. Ltd., China) with a UV cutoff filter ( 400 nm).The catalyst powders (0.10 g) were first dispersed on a circular glass dish equipped with a quartz window for passing light irradiation.Prior to photoreduction experiments, the reaction setup was vacuum-treated and then, high purity CO 2 gas (99.995) as purging and reaction gas was introduced into the reaction system for several times.The compressed high purity CO 2 gas was passed through a water bubbler to generate a mixture of CO 2 and H 2 O vapor.The distance between the sample and the light source was fixed at 11 or 30 cm to tune the irradiate intensity.After illumination, the gaseous products from the reactor were quantifiably identified for offline analysis using a GC-7890II gas chromatograph (Techcomp Corp., China) equipped with a flame ionized detector (FID) and GC-MS (Agilent 7890A-5975C) with a thermal conductivity detector (TCD).The equipped columns were TDX-01 and Porapak-Q, respectively.Other hydrocarbons were analyzed by GC-MS equipped with a DB-FFAP capillary column. Blank tests were also conducted in the absence of photocatalysts or in the dark with catalysts.No product was detected, indicating that the presence of both the visible-light irradiation and photocatalyst are indispensable for the photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 with water.To evaluate the stability, sample 7.4-BiOIg-C 3 N 4 as a model was refreshed by washing with deionized water and its photocatalytic performance was reevaluated in the light of the aforementioned procedure.Simultaneously, contrast tests, including using the mechanical mixture samples or mixing the 7.4-BiOIg-C 3 N 4 sample with edetate disodium salt (EDTA) to scavenge the photogenerated holes, were carried out to prove the role of the heterojunctions or to verify the charge transfer mode experimentally. 2.4.Analysis of the Intermediate H 2 O 2 .The formation of H 2 O 2 on the photocatalysts was detected by the UV-vis spectrum using otolidine as peroxide indicator. 48It has been proved that the production of H 2 O 2 can be monitored by measuring the absorption peak of the 2electron oxidation product of o-tolidine at 447 nm.The detection procedures were similar to the above photocatalytic tests, except that the catalysts were replaced by the mixture of the catalyst and o-tolidine (1:1.2 weight ratios).Sampling was carried out at given time intervals and then, the obtained samples were dispersed in deionized water.The suspension was filtered after centrifugation and the resulting solution was set to the same condition for UV-vis spectrum measurements of the 2-electron oxidized toluidine by T6 UV-vis spectrophotometer (Purkinje General, Beijing, China). 2.5.Analysis of the Intermediate OH.The formation of hydroxyl radicals ( OH) was detected by the PL technique using terephthalic acid (TA) as a probe molecule.It has been proven that TA reacts readily with OH to produce 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid (TAOH) with a typical fluorescent signal at 425 nm, and the intensity of the TAOH peak is proportional to the amount of hydroxyl radicals formed. 49The detection procedures were similar to the photocatalytic experiments, except that the catalysts were replaced by the mixture of the catalyst and TA (1:1.2 weight ratio).Sampling was carried out at given irradiation time intervals, and then, the obtained samples were dispersed in water.The resulting solution after centrifugal separation was set to a same concentration for fluorescence spectral measurements.The PL peak of TAOH at 425 nm was detected using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi F-7000) with excitation at 315 nm.

catalyst.source: A series of x-BiOIg-C 3 N 4 photocatalysts were synthesized by a simple deposition method.
catalyst.context: A series of x-BiOIg-C 3 N 4 photocatalysts were synthesized by a simple deposition method.The optimal 7.4-BiOIg-C 3 N 4 composite is proven to be good photocatalyst for CO 2 reduction and exhibits more efficient photocatalytic performance than pure BiOI and g-C 3 N 4 under visible light ( 400 nm).The enhanced photoactivity is attributed to the efficient separation of the photogenerated charge carriers.An indirect Z-scheme charge transfer mode is demonstrated by checking the redox I 3 -I -pair.On the basis of the final products and the intermediate OH and H 2 O 2 , a potential mechanism is further proposed.In summary, the present research is expected to be useful in developing Z-scheme photocatalysts and provide some meaningful information for deep understanding how the photogenerated electrons transfer across the solid-solid contact interface. light source.source: In this study, a series of Z-scheme BiOIg-C 3 N 4 photocatalysts are synthesized and their photocatalytic performance for CO 2 reduction to produce CO, H 2 andor CH 4 is evaluated under visible light irradiation ( 400 nm).
light source.context: Rational design and construction of Z-scheme photocatalysts has received much attention in the field of CO 2 reduction because of its great potential to solve the current energy and environmental crises.In this study, a series of Z-scheme BiOIg-C 3 N 4 photocatalysts are synthesized and their photocatalytic performance for CO 2 reduction to produce CO, H 2 andor CH 4 is evaluated under visible light irradiation ( 400 nm).The results show that the as-synthesized composites exhibit more highly efficient photocatalytic activity than pure g-C 3 N 4 and BiOI and that the product yields change remarkably depending on the reaction conditions such as irradiation light wavelength.Emphasis is placed on identifying how the charge transfers across the heterojunctions and an indirect Z-scheme charge transfer mechanism is verified by detecting the intermediate I 3-ions.The reaction mechanism is further proposed based on the detection of the intermediate OH and H 2 O 2 .This work may be useful for rationally designing of new types of Z-scheme photocatalyst and provide some illuminating insights into the Z-scheme transfer mechanism.

light source.source: Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) data was obtained with HeI (21.22 eV) as monochromatic light source.
light source.context: 2.1.Catalyst Preparation.All chemicals used in our experiments were of analytical reagent grade without further purification.Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C 3 N 4 ) was prepared according to the literature method. 47The hybridized BiOIg-C 3 N 4 composites were synthesized by a simple deposition method.In a typical experimental procedure, a quantitative of Bi(NO 3 ) 3 5H 2 O and g-C 3 N 4 (0.170 g) was added to 70.0 mL of glycol, according to the weight contents of BiOI in the composites with the percentage of 5, 8, 10, 11, 15, and 40 wt, respectively.After the suspension was ultrasonically dispersed for 30 min, a solution of KI in 95 alcohol (0.050 molL -1 ) was added dropwise and stirred for 24 h at room temperature.Samples with different ratio of BiOIg-C 3 N 4 were thus obtained after drying at 60 C for 12 h.To verify the real ratio of BiOIg-C 3 N 4 in the final composites, thermogravimetric analysis was employed to determine the content of BiOI.The results are displayed in Figure S1 and Table S1.The calculated BiOI contents are 2.5, 5.2, 7.4, 10.1, 14.3, and 38.9 wt , respectively.The calculated values are smaller than the nominal ones, indicating that a small part of BiOI was lost in the deposition process.The resulting specimens are labeled as x-BiOIg-C 3 N 4 , where x stands for the BiOI wt in the samples.For comparison, we also prepared pure BiOI and g-C 3 N 4 under the same conditions. 2.2.Catalyst Characterization.Thermal decomposition of the x-BiOIg-C 3 N 4 composites was monitored by thermogravimetric and differential scanning calorimeter (TG-DSC) with instrument NETZSCH STA 449C in a flow of air with heating rate of 5 C min in alumina crucible up to 750 C.The crystal phases of the samples were recorded with X-ray diffractometer (PANalytical X' pert PRO, Netherlands).Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was obtained on a Tecnai G 2 F20 S-TWIN electron microscope.Further, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) was employed.The PL spectra of samples were detected using a Hitachi F-4600 spectrometer.Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms were collected on a NOVA 1000e surface area and porosity analyzer (Quantachrome, Boynton Beach, FL). Chemical components and valence states were analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) on a Thermo Scientific ESCALab 250Xi Spectrometer with Al K radiation as the excitation source.Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) data was obtained with HeI (21.22 eV) as monochromatic light source.UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were performed on a scan UV-vis spectrometer (Cary 5000) with integrating sphere detector.The Raman spectra were obtained using a Renishaw inVia spectrophotometer.Photoelectrochemical tests in a conventional three-electrode system were recorded by a CHI 660E electrochemical workstation (Chenhua, Shanghai, China).The photocurrents of the photocatalysts were measured at 0.0 V (vs.AgAgCl) in Na 2 SO 4 aqueous solution under UV-visible light with AM 1.5 filter after being purged by N 2 to remove O 2 . 2.3.Photocatalytic Performance Tests.Photocatalytic activity for CO 2 reduction of the synthesized catalysts was evaluated in gaseous phase in a stainless steel cylindrical vessel with 15 cm length and 180 mL volume.The light source was a 300 W xenon arc lamp (PLS-SXE 300, Beijing Trust tech Co. Ltd., China) with a UV cutoff filter ( 400 nm).The catalyst powders (0.10 g) were first dispersed on a circular glass dish equipped with a quartz window for passing light irradiation.Prior to photoreduction experiments, the reaction setup was vacuum-treated and then, high purity CO 2 gas (99.995) as purging and reaction gas was introduced into the reaction system for several times.The compressed high purity CO 2 gas was passed through a water bubbler to generate a mixture of CO 2 and H 2 O vapor.The distance between the sample and the light source was fixed at 11 or 30 cm to tune the irradiate intensity.After illumination, the gaseous products from the reactor were quantifiably identified for offline analysis using a GC-7890II gas chromatograph (Techcomp Corp., China) equipped with a flame ionized detector (FID) and GC-MS (Agilent 7890A-5975C) with a thermal conductivity detector (TCD).The equipped columns were TDX-01 and Porapak-Q, respectively.Other hydrocarbons were analyzed by GC-MS equipped with a DB-FFAP capillary column. Blank tests were also conducted in the absence of photocatalysts or in the dark with catalysts.No product was detected, indicating that the presence of both the visible-light irradiation and photocatalyst are indispensable for the photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 with water.To evaluate the stability, sample 7.4-BiOIg-C 3 N 4 as a model was refreshed by washing with deionized water and its photocatalytic performance was reevaluated in the light of the aforementioned procedure.Simultaneously, contrast tests, including using the mechanical mixture samples or mixing the 7.4-BiOIg-C 3 N 4 sample with edetate disodium salt (EDTA) to scavenge the photogenerated holes, were carried out to prove the role of the heterojunctions or to verify the charge transfer mode experimentally. 2.4.Analysis of the Intermediate H 2 O 2 .The formation of H 2 O 2 on the photocatalysts was detected by the UV-vis spectrum using otolidine as peroxide indicator. 48It has been proved that the production of H 2 O 2 can be monitored by measuring the absorption peak of the 2electron oxidation product of o-tolidine at 447 nm.The detection procedures were similar to the above photocatalytic tests, except that the catalysts were replaced by the mixture of the catalyst and o-tolidine (1:1.2 weight ratios).Sampling was carried out at given time intervals and then, the obtained samples were dispersed in deionized water.The suspension was filtered after centrifugation and the resulting solution was set to the same condition for UV-vis spectrum measurements of the 2-electron oxidized toluidine by T6 UV-vis spectrophotometer (Purkinje General, Beijing, China). 2.5.Analysis of the Intermediate OH.The formation of hydroxyl radicals ( OH) was detected by the PL technique using terephthalic acid (TA) as a probe molecule.It has been proven that TA reacts readily with OH to produce 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid (TAOH) with a typical fluorescent signal at 425 nm, and the intensity of the TAOH peak is proportional to the amount of hydroxyl radicals formed. 49The detection procedures were similar to the photocatalytic experiments, except that the catalysts were replaced by the mixture of the catalyst and TA (1:1.2 weight ratio).Sampling was carried out at given irradiation time intervals, and then, the obtained samples were dispersed in water.The resulting solution after centrifugal separation was set to a same concentration for fluorescence spectral measurements.The PL peak of TAOH at 425 nm was detected using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi F-7000) with excitation at 315 nm.

light source.source: The optimal 7.4-BiOIg-C 3 N 4 composite is proven to be good photocatalyst for CO 2 reduction and exhibits more efficient photocatalytic performance than pure BiOI and g-C 3 N 4 under visible light ( 400 nm).
light source.context: A series of x-BiOIg-C 3 N 4 photocatalysts were synthesized by a simple deposition method.The optimal 7.4-BiOIg-C 3 N 4 composite is proven to be good photocatalyst for CO 2 reduction and exhibits more efficient photocatalytic performance than pure BiOI and g-C 3 N 4 under visible light ( 400 nm).The enhanced photoactivity is attributed to the efficient separation of the photogenerated charge carriers.An indirect Z-scheme charge transfer mode is demonstrated by checking the redox I 3 -I -pair.On the basis of the final products and the intermediate OH and H 2 O 2 , a potential mechanism is further proposed.In summary, the present research is expected to be useful in developing Z-scheme photocatalysts and provide some meaningful information for deep understanding how the photogenerated electrons transfer across the solid-solid contact interface. (HRTEM) and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) was employed.The PL spectra of samples were detected using a Hitachi F-4600 spectrometer.Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms were collected on a NOVA 1000e surface area and porosity analyzer (Quantachrome, Boynton Beach, FL). Chemical components and valence states were analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) on a Thermo Scientific ESCALab 250Xi Spectrometer with Al K radiation as the excitation source.Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) data was obtained with HeI (21.22 eV) as monochromatic light source.UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were performed on a scan UV-vis spectrometer (Cary 5000) with integrating sphere detector.The Raman spectra were obtained using a Renishaw inVia spectrophotometer.Photoelectrochemical tests in a conventional three-electrode system were recorded by a CHI 660E electrochemical workstation (Chenhua, Shanghai, China).The photocurrents of the photocatalysts were measured at 0.0 V (vs.AgAgCl) in Na 2 SO 4 aqueous solution under UV-visible light with AM 1.5 filter after being purged by N 2 to remove O 2 . 2.3.Photocatalytic Performance Tests.Photocatalytic activity for CO 2 reduction of the synthesized catalysts was evaluated in gaseous phase in a stainless steel cylindrical vessel with 15 cm length and 180 mL volume.The light source was a 300 W xenon arc lamp (PLS-SXE 300, Beijing Trust tech Co. Ltd., China) with a UV cutoff filter ( 400 nm).The catalyst powders (0.10 g) were first dispersed on a circular glass dish equipped with a quartz window for passing light irradiation.Prior to photoreduction experiments, the reaction setup was vacuum-treated and then, high purity CO 2 gas (99.995) as purging and reaction gas was introduced into the reaction system for several times.The compressed high purity CO 2 gas was passed through a water bubbler to generate a mixture of CO 2 and H 2 O vapor.The distance between the sample and the light source was fixed at 11 or 30 cm to tune the irradiate intensity.After illumination, the gaseous products from the reactor were quantifiably identified for offline analysis using a GC-7890II gas chromatograph (Techcomp Corp., China) equipped with a flame ionized detector (FID) and GC-MS (Agilent 7890A-5975C) with a thermal conductivity detector (TCD).The equipped columns were TDX-01 and Porapak-Q, respectively.Other hydrocarbons were analyzed by GC-MS equipped with a DB-FFAP capillary column. Blank tests were also conducted in the absence of photocatalysts or in the dark with catalysts.No product was detected, indicating that the presence of both the visible-light irradiation and photocatalyst are indispensable for the photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 with water.To evaluate the stability, sample 7.4-BiOIg-C 3 N 4 as a model was refreshed by washing with deionized water and its photocatalytic performance was reevaluated in the light of the aforementioned procedure.Simultaneously, contrast tests, including using the mechanical mixture samples or mixing the 7.4-BiOIg-C 3 N 4 sample with edetate disodium salt (EDTA) to scavenge the photogenerated holes, were carried out to prove the role of the heterojunctions or to verify the charge transfer mode experimentally. 2.4.Analysis of the Intermediate H 2 O 2 .The formation of H 2 O 2 on the photocatalysts was detected by the UV-vis spectrum using otolidine as peroxide indicator. 48It has been proved that the production of H 2 O 2 can be monitored by measuring the absorption peak of the 2electron oxidation product of o-tolidine at 447 nm.The detection procedures were similar to the above photocatalytic tests, except that the catalysts were replaced by the mixture of the catalyst and o-tolidine (1:1.2 weight ratios).Sampling was carried out at given time intervals and then, the obtained samples were dispersed in deionized water.The suspension was filtered after centrifugation and the resulting solution was set to the same condition for UV-vis spectrum measurements of the 2-electron oxidized toluidine by T6 UV-vis spectrophotometer (Purkinje General, Beijing, China). 2.5.Analysis of the Intermediate OH.The formation of hydroxyl radicals ( OH) was detected by the PL technique using terephthalic acid (TA) as a probe molecule.It has been proven that TA reacts readily with OH to produce 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid (TAOH) with a typical fluorescent signal at 425 nm, and the intensity of the TAOH peak is proportional to the amount of hydroxyl radicals formed. 49The detection procedures were similar to the photocatalytic experiments, except that the catalysts were replaced by the mixture of the catalyst and TA (1:1.2 weight ratio).Sampling was carried out at given irradiation time intervals, and then, the obtained samples were dispersed in water.The resulting solution after centrifugal separation was set to a same concentration for fluorescence spectral measurements.The PL peak of TAOH at 425 nm was detected using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi F-7000) with excitation at 315 nm.

operation mode.source: Figure 7. Yield of products on the number of batch runs for 7.4-BiOI g-C 3 N 4 under visible light irradiation for 3 h.
operation mode.context: A series of x-BiOIg-C 3 N 4 photocatalysts were synthesized by a simple deposition method.The optimal 7.4-BiOIg-C 3 N 4 composite is proven to be good photocatalyst for CO 2 reduction and exhibits more efficient photocatalytic performance than pure BiOI and g-C 3 N 4 under visible light ( 400 nm).The enhanced photoactivity is attributed to the efficient separation of the photogenerated charge carriers.An indirect Z-scheme charge transfer mode is demonstrated by checking the redox I 3 -I -pair.On the basis of the final products and the intermediate OH and H 2 O 2 , a potential mechanism is further proposed.In summary, the present research is expected to be useful in developing Z-scheme photocatalysts and provide some meaningful information for deep understanding how the photogenerated electrons transfer across the solid-solid contact interface.
